pages.full_text:
The dahlia walk: __Biddulph Grange Garden__ Staffordshire

Saturday October 5th 2002 Simon, Eve & Tonic left home at about 11 & motored north. We had sandwiches in the carpark of __Biddulph__ Grange Garden.
photo_captions: The dahlia walk: Biddulph Grange Garden Staffordshire
pages.notes: Page is dominated by a large colour photograph of the dahlia walk at Biddulph Grange Garden, Staffordshire. The photo caption is handwritten diagonally across the bottom-right of the photo. Text below is the start of the diary entry for Saturday October 5th 2002. No handwritten page number visible in the top-left corner. 'Tonic' appears to be a pet (dog) name. The underline on 'Biddulph' in the final sentence is uncertain — it may be the garden name underlined as a heading; transcribed accordingly.

pages.full_text:
It has hardly raind for a month. We walked round the Garden dry, but it's raining quite heavily later in the afternoon. This is one of the most interesting gardens that I have seen. It was designed in the 1850/60s, when plant collectors were bringing back specimens & Darwin wrote "The origin of species". The family were related to the Egerton-Warburtons of Arley Hall.
  The garden is divided into different sections, different microclimates, with follies providing connecting tunnels. We saw the dahlia walk in all its October splendour - it looked just like the postcard. We also saw the Chinese, Indian & Egyptian gardens, & a magnificent pinetum. The gardeners had used upsidedown pine-tree root stumps as interesting ground cover. We finally found the verbena parterre & there was a beautiful smooth velvety-leaved plant which smelled of lemon. A kind, wicked N.T. volunteer stole a leaf for me to use as
pages.notes: Page continues from left page; no explicit page number visible in top-right corner (possibly cropped). 'Staffordshire' is written vertically in the left margin (bleed from left page or repeated label). 'raind' is the diary's spelling (not 'rained'). 'Darwin' written as 'Darwin' — the diarist spells it 'Darwin' but the word 'Darwin' here refers to Charles Darwin. 'Egerton-Warburtons' is the family surname; 'Arley Hall' is in Cheshire. 'N.T.' = National Trust. Text ends mid-sentence, continuing to the next spread.
